definitions.text: That which was stated before, the aforesaid, the above, the same, likewise.

definitions.text: (auxiliary) A syntactic marker.
To perform; to execute.

extraExamples.definition: To cheat or swindle.

definitions.text: Not appropriate, of manners etc.
definitions.example: It is bad manners to talk with your mouth full.

extraExamples.example: Lard is bad for you. Smoking is bad for you, too. Grapes are bad for dogs but not for humans.
extraExamples.definition: Unhealthy; liable to cause health problems.

extraExamples.example: I went to the hospital to see how my grandfather was doing. Unfortunately, he's in a bad state.
extraExamples.definition: Sickly, unhealthy, unwell.

extraExamples.example: I want it so badly.
extraExamples.definition: Very much; to a great degree.

extraExamples.example: Stop being bad, or you will get a spanking!
extraExamples.definition: Not behaving; behaving badly; misbehaving; mischievous or disobedient.

extraExamples.example: Divorce is usually a bad experience for everybody involved.
extraExamples.definition: Tricky; stressful; unpleasant.

extraExamples.example: I had a bad headlight.
extraExamples.definition: Faulty; not functional.

extraExamples.example: He suffered from ill treatment.
extraExamples.definition: Indicative of unkind or malevolent intentions; harsh, cruel.

extraExamples.example: These apples have gone bad.
extraExamples.definition: (of food) Spoiled, rotten, overripe.

extraExamples.example: ill manners; ill will
extraExamples.definition: Unpropitious, unkind, faulty, not up to reasonable standard.

extraExamples.example: Bad breath is not pleasant for anyone.
extraExamples.definition: (of breath) Malodorous; foul.

extraExamples.example: I've been ill with the flu for the past few days.
extraExamples.definition: Unwell in terms of health or physical condition; sick.

extraExamples.example: They were caught trying to pass bad coinage.
extraExamples.definition: False; counterfeit; illegitimate.

extraExamples.example: Seeing those pictures made me ill.
extraExamples.definition: Having an urge to vomit.

extraExamples.example: He's a bad gardener; everything he tries to grow ends up dying.
extraExamples.definition: Unskilled; of limited ability; not good.

extraExamples.example: I don't look bad in this dress, do I?
extraExamples.definition: Of poor physical appearance.

extraExamples.example: That band was ill.
extraExamples.definition: Extremely bad (bad enough to make one ill). Generally used indirectly with to be.

extraExamples.example: Man, that new car you bought is bad!
extraExamples.definition: Good, superlative, excellent, cool.

extraExamples.example: He is in bad need of a haircut.
extraExamples.definition: (of a need, want, or pain) Severe, urgent.

extraExamples.example: He gave me a bad check.
extraExamples.definition: (of a draft/check) Not covered by funds on account.

definitions.text: Unfavorable; negative; not good.
definitions.example: Abducting children is bad for mothers.

definitions.text: Not suitable or fitting.
definitions.example: Do you think it is a bad idea to confront him directly?

definitions.text: (except in set phrases) To be, become, betide.
definitions.example: Well worth thee, me friend.

definitions.text: Wealth, fortune, riches, property, possessions.
Having a value of; proper to be exchanged for.
definitions.example: A painting worth thousands.

extraExamples.example: This job is hardly worth the effort.
extraExamples.definition: Making a fair equivalent of, repaying or compensating.

definitions.text: An injury, such as a cut, stab, or tear, to a (usually external) part of the body.
To blow air through a wind instrument or horn to make a sound.
To cause (someone) to become breathless, as by a blow to the abdomen, or by physical exertion, running, etc.
definitions.example: The boxer was winded during round two.

extraExamples.example: It took a long time to get over the wound of that insult.
extraExamples.definition: A hurt to a person's feelings, reputation, prospects, etc.

extraExamples.example: The police officer wounded the suspect during the fight that ensued.
extraExamples.definition: To hurt or injure (someone) by cutting, piercing, or tearing the skin.

extraExamples.example: to wind thread on a spool or into a ball
extraExamples.definition: To turn coils of (a cord or something similar) around something.

extraExamples.example: Please wind that old-fashioned alarm clock.
extraExamples.definition: To tighten the spring of a clockwork mechanism such as that of a clock.

extraExamples.example: The actor's pride was wounded when the leading role went to his rival.
extraExamples.definition: To hurt (a person's feelings).

extraExamples.example: The hounds winded the game.
extraExamples.definition: To perceive or follow by scent.

extraExamples.example: Vines wind round a pole.  The river winds through the plain.
extraExamples.definition: To travel in a way that is not straight.

extraExamples.example: to wind a rope with twine
extraExamples.definition: To cover or surround with something coiled about.

definitions.text: To form something by passing lengths or strands of material over and under one another.
definitions.example: This loom weaves yarn into sweaters.

definitions.text: To spin a cocoon or a web.
definitions.example: Spiders weave beautiful but deadly webs.

definitions.text: To unite by close connection or intermixture.

extraExamples.example: to weave the plot of a story
extraExamples.definition: To compose creatively and intricately; to fabricate.

extraExamples.example: Woven kevlar is tough enough to be bulletproof.
extraExamples.definition: Fabricated by weaving.

definitions.text: Vengeance; revenge; persecution; punishment; consequence; trouble.
To place in or hang on a rack.
(except in dialects) Ruin; destruction.

extraExamples.example: Post-and-lintel construction racks easily.
extraExamples.definition: (structural engineering) Tending to shear a structure (that is, force it to move in different directions at different points).

definitions.text: To anger; to enrage.
Wrathful; wroth; very angry.
To cause something harmful; to afflict; to inflict; to harm or injury; to let out something harmful; .
definitions.example: She wreaked her anger on his car.

definitions.text: Revenge; vengeance; furious passion; resentment.
To chasten, or chastise/chastize, or castigate, or punish, or smite.
definitions.example: The criminal has been wreaked by the Judge to spend a year in prison.

definitions.text: The remains of something that has been severely damaged or worn down.
An event in which something is damaged through collision.
To destroy violently; to cause severe damage to something, to a point where it no longer works, or is useless.
definitions.example: He wrecked the car in a collision.

extraExamples.example: He was an emotional wreck after the death of his wife.
extraExamples.definition: Something or someone that has been ruined.

definitions.text: A partition in a water wheel by which the form of the buckets is determined.
The act of wresting; a wrench or twist; distortion.
A metal (formerly wooden) piece of some ploughs attached under the mouldboard (the curved blade that turns over the furrow) for clearing out the furrow; the mouldboard itself.

extraExamples.example: He wrested the remote control from my grasp and changed the channel.
extraExamples.definition: To obtain by pulling or violent force.

definitions.text: A device for pressing or compressing, especially for cider.
A powerful squeezing or twisting action.
definitions.example: I grasped his hand and gave it a grateful wring.

definitions.text: To extract (a liquid) from something wet, especially cloth, by squeezing and twisting it.
definitions.example: Put the berries into a cheesecloth and wring the juice into a bowl.

extraExamples.example: to wring one's hands (with worry, etc.)
extraExamples.definition: To hold (something) tightly and press or twist.

extraExamples.example: to wring a mast
extraExamples.definition: To bend or strain out of its position.

definitions.text: The complex joint between forearm bones, carpus, and metacarpals where the hand is attached to the arm; the carpus in a narrow sense.
A stud or pin which forms a journal.
The act or style of writing.
The operation of storing data, as in memory or onto disk.
definitions.example: How many writes per second can this hard disk handle?

definitions.text: To form letters, words or symbols on a surface in order to communicate.
definitions.example: The pupil wrote his name on the paper.

extraExamples.example: My uncle writes newspaper articles for The Herald.
extraExamples.definition: To be the author of (a book, article, poem, etc.).

extraExamples.example: (UK) Please write to me when you get there.
extraExamples.definition: To send written information to.

extraExamples.example: The due day of the homework is written in the syllabus.
extraExamples.definition: To show (information, etc) in written form.

extraExamples.example: The computer writes to the disk faster than it reads from it.
extraExamples.definition: (with to) To record data mechanically or electronically.

extraExamples.example: I was very anxious to know my score after I wrote the test.
extraExamples.definition: (of an exam, a document, etc.) To fill in, to complete using words.

extraExamples.example: truth written on the heart
extraExamples.definition: To impress durably; to imprint; to engrave.

definitions.text: Something that is immoral or not good.
definitions.example: Injustice is a heinous wrong.

definitions.text: An instance of wronging someone (sometimes with possessive to indicate the wrongdoer).
The incorrect or unjust position or opinion.

extraExamples.example: Some of your answers were correct, and some were wrong.
extraExamples.definition: Incorrect or untrue.

extraExamples.example: You're wrong: he's not Superman at all.
extraExamples.definition: Asserting something incorrect or untrue.

extraExamples.example: It is wrong to lie.
extraExamples.definition: Immoral, not good, bad.

extraExamples.example: A bikini is the wrong thing to wear on a cold day.
extraExamples.definition: Improper; unfit; unsuitable.

extraExamples.example: I spelled several names wrong in my address book.
extraExamples.definition: In a way that isn't right; incorrectly, wrongly.

extraExamples.example: Don't cry, honey. Tell me what's wrong.
extraExamples.definition: Not working; out of order.

definitions.text: To form letters, words or symbols on a surface in order to communicate.
definitions.example: The pupil wrote his name on the paper.

definitions.text: To be the author of (a book, article, poem, etc.).
definitions.example: My uncle writes newspaper articles for The Herald.

definitions.text: To send written information to.
definitions.example: (UK) Please write to me when you get there.

extraExamples.example: The due day of the homework is written in the syllabus.
extraExamples.definition: To show (information, etc) in written form.

extraExamples.example: The computer writes to the disk faster than it reads from it.
extraExamples.definition: (with to) To record data mechanically or electronically.

extraExamples.example: I was very anxious to know my score after I wrote the test.
extraExamples.definition: (of an exam, a document, etc.) To fill in, to complete using words.

extraExamples.example: truth written on the heart
extraExamples.definition: To impress durably; to imprint; to engrave.

definitions.text: To squeeze or twist (something) tightly so that liquid is forced out. See also wring out.
definitions.example: I didn’t have a towel so I just wrung my hair dry.

definitions.text: To extract (a liquid) from something wet, especially cloth, by squeezing and twisting it.
definitions.example: Put the berries into a cheesecloth and wring the juice into a bowl.

definitions.text: To obtain (something from or out of someone or something) by force.

extraExamples.example: to wring one's hands (with worry, etc.)
extraExamples.definition: To hold (something) tightly and press or twist.

extraExamples.example: to wring a mast
extraExamples.definition: To bend or strain out of its position.

definitions.text: A slick and light ship for making pleasure trips or racing on water, having sails but often motor-powered. At times used as a residence offshore on a dock.
definitions.example: Would you like to go sailing on my uncle’s yacht?

definitions.text: Any vessel used for private, noncommercial purposes.
To long, have a strong desire (for something).
definitions.example: All I yearn for is a simple life.

definitions.text: To long for something in the past with melancholy, nostalgically.
To have strong feelings of love, sympathy, affection, etc. (toward someone).
An often humid, yellowish froth produced by fermenting malt worts, and used to brew beer, leaven bread, and also used in certain medicines.
A single-celled fungus of a wide variety of taxonomic families.
(of something prepared with a yeasted dough) To rise.
To pay, give in payment; repay, recompense; reward; requite.
To furnish; to afford; to render; to give forth.
A product; the quantity of something produced.
definitions.example: Zucchini plants always seem to produce a high yield of fruit.

extraExamples.example: Yield the right of way to pedestrians.
extraExamples.definition: To give way; to allow another to pass first.

extraExamples.example: They refuse to yield to the enemy.
extraExamples.definition: To give as required; to surrender, relinquish or capitulate.

extraExamples.example: Historically, that security yields a high return.
extraExamples.definition: To produce as return, as from an investment.

extraExamples.example: Adding 3 and 4 yields a result of 7.
extraExamples.definition: To produce as a result.

extraExamples.example: Indo-European p- yields Germanic f-.
extraExamples.definition: To produce a particular sound as the result of a sound law.

definitions.text: (possibly nonstandard) An individual offspring; a single recently born or hatched organism.
To become or seem to become younger.
To cause to appear younger.

extraExamples.example: a lamb is a young sheep;  these picture books are for young readers
extraExamples.definition: In the early part of growth or life; born not long ago.

extraExamples.example: the age of space travel is still young;   a young business
extraExamples.definition: At an early stage of existence or development; having recently come into existence.

extraExamples.example: How young is your dog?   Her grandmother turned 70 years young last month.
extraExamples.definition: (Not) advanced in age; (far towards or) at a specified stage of existence or age.

extraExamples.example: The cynical world soon shattered my young dreams.
extraExamples.definition: Of or belonging to the early part of life.

definitions.text: The quality or state of being young.
definitions.example: Her youth and beauty attracted him to her.

definitions.text: The part of life following childhood; the period of existence preceding maturity or age; the whole early part of life, from childhood, or, sometimes, from infancy, to adulthood.
definitions.example: I made many mistakes in my youth, but learned from them all.

definitions.text: A young man; a male adolescent or young adult.
An unlikely diagnosis, especially for symptoms probably caused by a common ailment. (Originates in the advice often given to medical students: "when you hear hoofbeats, think of horses, not zebras".)
A biracial person, specifically one born to a member of the Sub-Saharan African race and a Caucasian.
Any of various papilionid butterflies of the subgenus Paranticopsis of the genus Graphium, having black and white markings.
Related to, associated with, or similar to zones.
(of air or ocean currents) roughly longitudinal (east to west, or west to east)
